evidence: Docstring: 'Let sigma_1(n) = sigma(n), the sum of divisors function' — the statement iterates the divisor function sigma.
The problem asks about lim sigma_k(n)^{1/k} for the iterates sigma_k(n) = sigma(sigma_{k-1}(n)); erdosproblems.com tags it 'iterated functions'.
Docstring: 'Let sigma_1(n)=sigma(n), the sum of divisors function' — the question concerns iterates of the divisor function.
Asks whether the iterated sequences sigma_i(m) and sigma_j(n) always meet; erdosproblems.com tags it 'iterated functions'.
Header: barriers are defined for 'omega, the number of distinct prime divisors'; parts.i asks whether infinitely many omega-barriers exist.
Parts.ii asks for epsilon > 0 with infinitely many n satisfying m + epsilon*omega(m) <= n, a relaxed barrier condition on the prime omega function.
Docstring: 'Erdos believed there should be infinitely many barriers for Omega, the total prime multiplicity' — Omega counted with multiplicity is the big prime omega function.
Docstring: 'Selfridge computed that the largest Omega-barrier below 10^5 is 99840', a statement about barriers of the prime omega function Omega.
The auxiliary function is h(n) = n + tau(n) 'where tau(n) counts the number of divisors of n' — the divisor-counting function tau.
Asks whether the iterates h_i(m) and h_j(n) always collide; erdosproblems.com tags problem 414 'iterated functions'.
V(x) counts n <= x such that phi(m) = n is solvable — the statement is about the value set of Euler's totient function.
Asks for an asymptotic formula for V(x), the counting function of totient values of Euler's totient function.
Pillai's theorem V(x) = o(x) about the number of values of phi; reference cites 'On some functions connected with phi(n)'.
Erdos's estimate V(x) = x(log x)^{-1+o(1)} for the count of totient values; reference cites 'Euler's varphi-function'.
Maier-Pomerance asymptotic for V(x); reference cites 'On the number of distinct values of Euler's phi-function'.
Ford's order-of-magnitude formula for V(x); reference cites 'The distribution of totients'.
V'(x) and V(x) both count values of phi(m) — the question is whether lim V(x)/V'(x) exists for Euler's totient function.
Asks whether the limit of the ratio of the two totient-value counting functions exceeds 1.
Docstring: 'Are there infinitely many integers not of the form n - phi(n)? ... Numbers not of the form we call non-cototients.'
The statement concerns integers not of the form n - phi(n), defined via Euler's totient function (Nat.totient in the Lean statement).
Docstring: 'It follows from a slight strengthening of the Goldbach conjecture that every odd number can be written as n - phi(n)'; the Lean statement takes a Goldbach-style hypothesis.
Docstring: numbers not of the form sigma(n)-n 'are called nonaliquot, or sometimes untouchable', and the statement asserts they have positive density.
Browkin-Schinzel solution: every 2^(k+1)*509203 is not of the form n - phi(n), i.e. is a non-cototient.
Docstring: 'It is open whether the set of non-cototients has positive density.'
The statement asks for the limit points of tau((n+1)!)/tau(n!) 'if tau(n) counts the number of divisors of n' — the divisor-counting function sigma_0.
The ratio is of divisor counts of consecutive factorials n! and (n+1)!; erdosproblems.com tags problem 419 'factorials'.
Docstring: asks whether every maximal Sidon set A in {1,...,N} admits another Sidon set B of size M with (A-A) and (B-B) meeting only in 0.
Constructive variant asking for an explicit bound f(M) on N for the maximal-Sidon-set disjoint-difference-set property.
